I agree with this claim because the Judet view's angled projection allows comprehensive imaging of the scaphoid bone — especially its proximal and distal ends — which are prone to injury due to their location and function during hand movements. This specific orientation helps detect early signs of fracture that may otherwise go unnoticed on routine radiographs.

Yes – I believe it’s correct as the Judet view is primarily designed for assessing scaphoid injuries, particularly those involving the proximal pole where small fragments can easily be overlooked.
